subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree, likely in Library and Information Science or a closely related field
Completed undergraduate studies before pursuing graduate education
Has participated in ongoing professional development workshops related to library technology and resource management
Attended industry conferences and seminars to remain current with library trends
Completed training in procurement and negotiation as part of professional development

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title is Library Technician with a specialization in acquisitions and purchasing
Role centers around evaluating, negotiating, and purchasing library resources and subscriptions
Responsible for managing orders, contracts, and vendor relationships
Coordinates with multiple departments for business, legal, and cybersecurity review of contracts
Produces reports and resource evaluations for internal stakeholders

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the library and information services sector
Employed by an academic or public library, likely within the education industry
Engages with both public and private sector vendors supplying library materials
Sector is characterized by rapid changes in digital resource availability and procurement models

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works for a mid-to-large sized organization, such as a university or municipal library system
Operates within a structured environment with established procurement and IT protocols
Participates in cross-departmental meetings and collaborations
Company environment supports professional development and technology adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Organization is part of the education and information management sector
Serves students, faculty, or the general public as primary end users
Engages in the acquisition of digital and print resources, including databases, journals, and books
Market segment includes partnerships with publishers, vendors, and consortia

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has over 10 years of experience in the library technician role
Likely progressed through more junior positions before current responsibilities
Experience spans significant changes in digital resource acquisition practices
Tenure has included participation in several system upgrades and process changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a mid-to-senior level position within the acquisitions unit
Acts as a subject matter expert on resource evaluation and purchasing
May serve as a mentor or trainer to more junior colleagues
Involved in decision-making processes impacting department workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Evaluates and negotiates purchase agreements for library resources
Manages orders from initial inquiry through contract finalization and receipt
Produces evaluations and financial reports on resource usage and value
Communicates frequently with vendors and internal staff via email and meetings
Handles paperwork processing, including order tracking, filing, and contract management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 25% of time on email communication and sorting
Another 25% is allocated to attending meetings with vendors and staff
25% is dedicated to producing reports and conducting evaluations
The remaining 25% covers paperwork, order processing, training, and miscellaneous administrative tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates with moderate autonomy in evaluating and recommending purchases
Has authority to negotiate terms with vendors within set organizational guidelines
Final approval for large purchases or contracts may require higher-level sign-off
Independently manages day-to-day tasks but collaborates on strategic decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Email communication and sorting is a routine, well-practiced task
Order processing and tracking follows established procedures and is straightforward
Filing paperwork is considered a low-complexity, habitual activity
Scheduling and attending regular meetings is familiar and manageable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Negotiating complex contracts with vendors can be intricate due to unique terms
Coordinating multi-departmental reviews involving legal and cybersecurity can be time-consuming
Evaluating new resource offerings requires in-depth analysis of value and user needs
Synthesizing data from disparate systems for detailed reporting is challenging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Sorting and filing incoming emails and documents is highly repetitive
Compiling order and usage data into spreadsheets for reports is data-heavy
Routine contract status updates in order management systems could be automated
Data validation and cross-checking between systems is repetitive and error-prone

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys resource evaluation and negotiation for the intellectual challenge
Finds meetings with vendors and collaborative solution-finding engaging
Paperwork filing and extensive data entry are considered tedious
Repetitive administrative tasks, like timesheet entry, are least enjoyable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Data is often siloed across multiple platforms, making consolidated analysis difficult
Manual entry and cross-checking increase the risk of errors
Waiting for multi-departmental approvals can cause delays in procurement
Email overload and sorting consume significant productive time

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Tasks that leverage analytical skills, such as contract evaluation, are both enjoyable and productive
High skill in routine administrative tasks boosts productivity but may not enhance enjoyment
Engagement increases when learning is involved, such as exploring new vendor offerings
Repetitive tasks, despite proficiency, can lead to disengagement over time

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally open to adopting new technologies that improve workflow efficiency
Appreciates practical training and hands-on demonstrations for new tools
Views learning new tech as a necessity in a changing industry
Prefers incremental introduction of new software rather than disruptive changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self moderately adaptable, with a preference for structured onboarding
Has successfully adapted to several software upgrades and new ordering systems over career
May experience initial hesitation but gains confidence after practice
Values clear documentation and support during transitions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to invest time in training if it’s directly relevant to core job duties
Attends occasional workshops and webinars, especially when mandated or strongly encouraged
Balances training time with workload, prioritizing urgent operational needs
Prefers short, focused sessions over lengthy, generic training

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential for increased efficiency and reduced repetitive work
Barriers include high workload and time constraints during peak periods
Relevance to current job tasks is a key motivator for engaging with training
Support from management and peers increases willingness to learn new systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peer recommendations and success stories positively influence adoption
Management endorsement and clear communication of benefits encourage engagement
Collaborative learning sessions with colleagues are preferred over isolated study
Reluctance to adopt may occur if there’s skepticism among team members

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Microsoft Outlook extensively for email management
Relies on Microsoft Teams for meetings and team communication
Handles files and collaboration through SharePoint
Regularly works in Excel for data analysis and reporting
Uses Word for document creation and editing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Utilizes specialized ordering systems for resource procurement
Employs PeopleSoft for business process management and financial tracking
Accesses internal intranet for policy documents and updates
Uses networked printers and copiers for paperwork processing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with standard Microsoft Office applications
Experienced with library management systems and vendor ordering platforms
Has used integrated financial and HR systems like PeopleSoft
Familiar with digital resource management and cataloging software

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Data silos between ordering systems and financial software cause duplicate entry
Integration between Excel and ordering platforms is often manual
Occasional compatibility issues when moving documents between SharePoint and other systems
Lack of unified dashboards complicates consolidated reporting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Has some experience using LLMs (Large Language Models) and basic AI tools
Understands core concepts such as natural language processing and automation
Familiar with AI applications in productivity and data analysis
Not a technical expert but keeps informed about major AI trends relevant to the field

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Has used ChatGPT or similar LLM-based tools for generating text drafts
Occasionally uses built-in AI features in Microsoft Office (e.g., Excel suggestions, Outlook smart replies)
Tested automated meeting note-takers or transcription services
No daily use of dedicated AI procurement tools, but open to experimentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs occasionally for drafting emails or summarizing meeting notes
Applies AI tools for quick data analysis or generating initial report drafts
Employs AI features on an as-needed basis, not as a core workflow component
Explores new AI tools when they promise clear time savings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used AI to draft a summary of a lengthy vendor contract, saving time
AI-generated email drafts required manual review for tone and accuracy
Encountered occasional errors in AI data extraction from complex spreadsheets
Learnt to double-check AI outputs before finalizing any communications or reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Some informal peer-to-peer sharing of AI tool tips within the team
Limited formal in-house training on AI; relies on online tutorials and documentation
Management encourages exploration but does not mandate AI tool use
Occasional webinars or info sessions offered by the organization on emerging tech

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools when learning new workflows
Comfortable working independently with familiar AI features
Appreciates AI systems that offer suggestions rather than take full control
Values transparency in how AI arrives at recommendations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant to reduce mundane or repetitive work
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor for data analysis or information retrieval
Does not rely on AI as a co-worker for high-stakes tasks requiring domain expertise
Prefers to retain final decision authority when using AI-generated outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to adopting AI-driven workflows if they clearly save time or reduce errors
More likely to adopt AI for support tasks than for primary decision-making
Willing to pilot new AI tools with appropriate training and support
Cautious about fully automating complex or sensitive processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for drafting and brainstorming tasks
Finds command-based interfaces efficient for routine, well-defined actions
Values flexibility to switch between interaction styles based on context
Appreciates AI tools that allow clarification and follow-up questions in natural language

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Resource evaluation and negotiation require significant domain and institutional knowledge
Contract review involves understanding organizational policies and legal nuances
Many decisions depend on experience with specific vendors and user needs
Routine administrative tasks require less specialized knowledge and are more amenable to automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for basic drafting, summarization, and data analysis if results are reviewed
Skeptical of AI handling legal or financial decisions without expert oversight
Willing to use AI outputs as a starting point but insists on manual verification
Trust increases with transparent AI systems that explain their reasoning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive, low-risk tasks like data entry and document formatting
Human oversight is essential for contract negotiation, vendor evaluation, and final approvals
Sees value in AI-generated recommendations but expects final decisions to remain human-led
Supports partial automation with clear override mechanisms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable for standardized, low-impact processes
Feels threatened by automation that could displace roles requiring judgment or institutional experience
Wants assurance of manual intervention options in automated workflows
Seeks clear communication on the scope and limits of AI automation

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI can enhance productivity by freeing time for higher-value analysis
Routine skills like data entry may become less important with automation
Analytical, negotiation, and domain-specific skills will remain critical
Sees a growing need for digital literacy and AI tool proficiency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Anticipates learning to supervise or validate AI-generated content
May take on responsibilities for AI tool selection and workflow integration
Will need to interpret AI-driven analytics for decision support
Growing expectation to assist peers with AI tool onboarding and troubleshooting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about automation of repetitive administrative tasks
Not worried about AI replacing core responsibilities requiring negotiation and judgment
Sees some risk in future if AI systems gain advanced analytical and contextual abilities
Emphasizes the continuing need for human oversight in resource management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Insists on manual review of AI-generated drafts and reports before distribution
Prefers systems that allow easy interruption or correction of automated workflows
Regularly double-checks data processed by AI for accuracy and completeness
Advocates for clear audit trails and accountability in AI-assisted processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has become more open to AI experimentation in recent years
No mandatory AI adoption, but growing encouragement from management
Policies emphasize responsible use and data privacy when using AI
Some uncertainty remains about long-term AI strategy and workforce impact

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Views AI as a significant force for efficiency and innovation in libraries
Recognizes potential for job transformation, especially in administrative roles
Believes AI can democratize access to information and streamline operations
Cautions that rapid change may outpace ethical and regulatory frameworks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports human oversight to prevent bias and errors in AI outputs
Worried about data privacy and the protection of sensitive information
Believes transparency and accountability are essential in AI deployment
Advocates for inclusive policies to ensure fair access to AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools that can perform complex, cross-system data analysis and reporting
Would benefit from an AI-powered meeting note-taker that integrates directly with SharePoint
Sees value in AI that can intelligently sort and prioritize incoming emails
Wants customizable AI workflows for contract review and compliance checking

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine administrative work to become increasingly automated
Predicts a shift toward more strategic analysis and vendor relationship management
Foresees a need to continually update technical and analytical skills
Believes collaboration with AI will become a standard part of the profession

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Advocates for structured AI training programs tailored to library workflows
Recommends regular review and updating of data privacy and ethics guidelines
Encourages pilot projects to test new AI tools before full-scale adoption
Wants clear communication about the impact of AI on roles and responsibilities
